Keywords: package.json, name, version, main, scripts, entry point
Description: The package.json file is the manifest for every Node.js project; it declares the package name, version, entry points, scripts, and dependencies.
Do: Always include "name", "version", "main" (or "exports"), "scripts", and "license" fields. Keep scripts concise and use conventional names like "build", "test", "start", and "lint".
Dont: Do not omit the "name" or "version" fields for published packages. Do not put build commands directly in "start"; keep "start" for running the production server.

Description: Lockfiles pin every dependency to an exact resolved version, ensuring deterministic installs across machines and CI environments.
Do: Always commit your lockfile to version control. Use "npm ci" or "pnpm install --frozen-lockfile" in CI to guarantee reproducible builds.
Dont: Do not delete the lockfile to "fix" install issues; run "npm install" or "pnpm install" to regenerate it properly. Do not mix package managers in the same project.

Keywords: semver, caret, tilde, exact version, version range, ^, ~
Description: Semantic versioning (semver) uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH to communicate the impact of changes; npm range operators like ^ and ~ control which updates are accepted.
Do: Use ^ (caret) for libraries to accept backward-compatible updates. Pin exact versions for critical production dependencies where even minor updates could cause breakage.
Dont: Do not use * or "latest" as a version range in production dependencies. Do not ignore major version bumps; they signal breaking changes.

Keywords: peerDependencies, plugin, host package, version conflict
Description: Peer dependencies declare that a package is compatible with a specific version of a host package without bundling it, commonly used by plugins and frameworks.
Do: Use peerDependencies when your package is a plugin or addon that relies on a host package the consumer already installs. Specify a wide but tested version range.
Dont: Do not put peer dependencies in regular dependencies; it causes duplicate installations. Do not specify an overly narrow peer range that forces consumers to use one exact version.

Keywords: turborepo, turbo, monorepo, workspace, pipeline, cache
Description: Turborepo orchestrates tasks across monorepo workspaces with intelligent caching, parallel execution, and topological task ordering.
Do: Define a "pipeline" in turbo.json specifying task dependencies and outputs. Use remote caching in CI to share build artifacts across machines.
Dont: Do not skip defining "outputs" in turbo.json; without them, Turborepo cannot cache correctly. Do not run tasks outside of turbo if they depend on other workspace builds.

Keywords: nx, monorepo, project graph, affected, cache, plugins
Description: Nx is a monorepo build system that uses a project graph to determine task dependencies, supports computation caching, and runs only affected tasks on changes.
Do: Use "nx affected" in CI to run only the tasks impacted by the current changeset. Configure cacheableOperations and leverage Nx plugins for framework-specific optimizations.
Dont: Do not run all tasks on every commit; use "nx affected" to save CI time. Do not ignore the project graph; misconfigured dependencies lead to stale caches.

Keywords: npm workspaces, monorepo, workspace protocol, hoisting
Description: npm workspaces provide native monorepo support in npm 7+, allowing you to manage multiple packages from a single root package.json.
Do: Declare workspaces in the root package.json. Use "npm run -w <pkg>" to run scripts in a specific workspace and "npm install -w <pkg> <dep>" to add dependencies.
Dont: Do not install dependencies from the individual package directories; always install from the root. Do not forget to set "private": true in the root package.json.

Keywords: pnpm workspaces, pnpm-workspace.yaml, workspace protocol, strict
Description: pnpm workspaces use a pnpm-workspace.yaml file and the "workspace:" protocol to link local packages in a monorepo with strict dependency isolation.
Do: Create a pnpm-workspace.yaml at the root listing workspace directories. Use "workspace:*" protocol to reference sibling packages so they resolve locally.
Dont: Do not use file: or link: protocols for local packages; use workspace:* instead. Do not forget to create pnpm-workspace.yaml or pnpm will not recognize workspaces.
Example:
# pnpm-workspace.yaml
packages:
  - "packages/*"
  - "apps/*"

# In a package's package.json:
# "dependencies": { "@myorg/shared": "workspace:*" }

Keywords: esm, cjs, commonjs, es modules, interop, require, import, dual package
Description: Node.js supports both ECMAScript Modules (ESM) and CommonJS (CJS); interoperability requires careful configuration of package.json "type" field and file extensions.
Do: Set "type": "module" for ESM projects. Use .mjs/.cjs extensions when mixing module systems. For libraries, ship both ESM and CJS builds via the "exports" field.
Dont: Do not use require() in ESM files without createRequire. Do not assume named imports work when importing CJS from ESM; use default import and destructure.

Keywords: tree shaking, dead code elimination, side effects, esm, bundler
Description: Tree shaking is a bundler optimization that eliminates unused exports from the final bundle, reducing output size significantly.
Do: Mark packages as side-effect-free with "sideEffects": false in package.json. Write pure ESM exports so bundlers can statically analyze imports.
Dont: Do not use CommonJS require() in library code intended for tree shaking; bundlers cannot statically analyze CJS. Do not put side effects at the module top level without declaring them.

Keywords: exports, conditional exports, subpath exports, package.json, entry points
Description: The "exports" field in package.json defines conditional and subpath entry points, controlling which files consumers can import and under which conditions.
Do: Use "exports" to expose specific entry points for ESM, CJS, and TypeScript types. Define subpath exports for feature-specific imports like "my-pkg/utils".
Dont: Do not rely solely on "main" for modern packages; "exports" takes precedence in Node.js 12.11+. Do not expose internal files without an explicit subpath export.

Keywords: types, typings, typescript, declarations, d.ts, type definitions
Description: The "types" (or "typings") field in package.json points to the main TypeScript declaration file so consumers get type information without the source code.
Do: Set "types" to the path of your generated .d.ts file. When using the "exports" field, include a "types" condition in each export entry for proper resolution.
Dont: Do not forget to generate .d.ts files during the build step. Do not point "types" to a .ts source file; it must reference the compiled .d.ts declaration file.

Description: dependencies are packages required at runtime; devDependencies are packages needed only during development, testing, or building.
Do: Put runtime libraries (express, prisma) in dependencies. Put build tools, linters, test frameworks, and type definitions in devDependencies.
Dont: Do not put build-only tools like TypeScript, ESLint, or Vitest in dependencies. Do not put runtime-required packages in devDependencies or production installs will fail.

Keywords: npm publish, prepublish, package files, registry, tarball
Description: Publishing to npm makes a package available to the community; a proper prepublish checklist ensures quality, correct contents, and proper metadata.
Do: Run "npm pack --dry-run" to verify included files before publishing. Set the "files" field to whitelist only dist/ and necessary assets. Always build and test before publish.
Dont: Do not publish without reviewing the tarball contents. Do not include src/, tests, or config files in the published package. Do not forget to bump the version before publishing.

Keywords: private, workspace package, no publish, internal
Description: Setting "private": true in package.json prevents a package from being accidentally published to the npm registry; commonly used for app packages and monorepo roots.
Do: Set "private": true for application packages, monorepo roots, and any workspace package that should never be published.
Dont: Do not leave off "private": true on internal packages; an accidental "npm publish" could expose proprietary code to the public registry.

Keywords: npm scripts, lifecycle scripts, pre, post, run
Description: npm scripts defined in package.json replace the need for task runners; lifecycle hooks like pre/post automatically run before/after a named script.
Do: Use conventional script names (build, test, start, lint, format). Leverage pre/post hooks for setup and cleanup. Keep scripts focused and composable.
Dont: Do not chain too many commands with && in a single script; break them into named scripts. Do not use OS-specific commands (rm -rf) without cross-env or rimraf for cross-platform support.

Keywords: engines, node version, npm version, constraints, semver range
Description: The "engines" field in package.json specifies which versions of Node.js and npm the package is compatible with, helping prevent runtime errors.
Do: Set "engines" to the minimum Node.js version your code requires. Use "engine-strict" config or a .npmrc with engine-strict=true to enforce it during install.
Dont: Do not set an overly restrictive engines range that blocks consumers unnecessarily. Do not forget to actually test on the minimum version you declare.

Keywords: overrides, resolutions, dependency forcing, version pinning, security patch
Description: overrides (npm) and resolutions (yarn/pnpm) let you force specific versions of transitive dependencies, often used to patch security vulnerabilities or resolve conflicts.
Do: Use overrides to patch a known vulnerability in a transitive dependency until the direct dependency releases a fix. Document every override with a comment or linked issue.
Dont: Do not leave stale overrides in package.json after the upstream fix is released. Do not use overrides to force incompatible major versions; it can cause subtle runtime errors.
